title: SEGMENT 4 — How GFP actually glows: the barrel and the self-cooking chromophore

lines.text: SEGMENT 4 — How GFP actually glows: the barrel and the self-cooking chromophore
If I could show you only one picture today, it would be the structure of GFP.
So close your eyes for a moment and let me build it for you in words.
Here's the analogy first, because it will guide the whole segment.
Picture an aluminum soda can — closed, sealed, watertight — with a single fluorescent ingredient sealed inside.
The metal can is the protein.
The fluorescent ingredient is something the can's contents cook up for themselves once the can is sealed shut.
That's GFP.
The outer protein wraps around like a can, the glowing part forms in the center, and the wrapper protects the glow from the watery cytoplasm outside.
Let me put real numbers and shapes on that.
GFP is a relatively small protein — about 238 amino acids long, which makes it a moderately-sized protein.
When those amino acids fold up, they organize into a very distinctive shape called an eleven-stranded beta barrel.

Picture a hollow tube made of eleven flat, ribbon-like strands of protein, each one lying alongside the next, held together by hydrogen bonds along their edges, closing back around to form a closed cylinder.
The cylinder is about four nanometers tall and two and a half nanometers across.
The walls are tight and complete.
Almost nothing in solution can get in or out.
People sometimes call this fold the "beta-can" because it really does look like a sealed cylindrical container — a soda can made of protein.
Now, running right down the central axis of the barrel, there's a single short alpha helix threaded through the middle of the cylinder.
And on that internal helix, sitting at the geometric heart of the barrel, are three specific amino acids at positions 65, 66, and 67 of the chain: a serine, a tyrosine, and a glycine.
Ser-Tyr-Gly.
Those three amino acids, sealed inside the barrel, are going to undergo a chemical reaction that turns them into the chromophore — the colored, glowing part of the molecule.
Here's where the analogy gets fun.
The chromophore is, in a sense, a self-cooking ingredient.

Once the protein folds and the three amino acids are sealed in the middle, three spontaneous chemical steps happen, with no enzyme, no helper, nothing — just chemistry that the geometry forces to occur.
Step one: the protein backbone bends just so, such that the glycine's nitrogen reaches over and attacks the serine's carbonyl carbon, forming a new five-membered ring that wasn't there a moment ago.
Step two: a single water molecule gets squeezed out — chemists call this dehydration.

Step three: the tyrosine side chain, which has an aromatic ring, gets oxidized by molecular oxygen — the very oxygen the cell breathes — and a new double bond forms that hooks the tyrosine's ring into the new ring from step one.
The end result is one extended, conjugated electronic system — a long string of double bonds running through both rings.
And it's that extended electronic system that absorbs blue light and re-emits green.
That's the chromophore.
That's what makes GFP glow.
Now read what just happened, because the implication is enormous.
The chromophore made itself.
No enzyme came along to assemble it.
The protein folded, the three internal residues found themselves in the right geometry, and they reacted with each other and with ambient oxygen to form the fluorescent group, all on their own.
This is autocatalytic chromophore maturation, and it is the single fact that makes GFP work in every organism it's ever been put into.
There's no jellyfish-specific factor that has to come along.
There's just the protein, the oxygen the cell is already breathing, and time.
Give a cell the GFP gene, let it translate the protein, let it fold, wait a few minutes to a few hours, and the chromophore matures and the protein lights up.
The oxygen requirement is worth pausing on.
GFP needs oxygen to mature.
If you try to use it in cells starved of oxygen — strict anaerobic bacteria, the hypoxic core of a tumor — the chromophore can't form, and you get dark protein.
That's a real limitation, and we'll come back to it briefly in the final segment when we talk about oxygen-independent alternatives.
But for most ordinary cell biology, oxygen is around, and the cooking just happens.
Now, the other half of the picture: why the barrel?
Why does this elaborate cylindrical shell wrap the chromophore?
Couldn't the chromophore just float in solution and glow?
Here's the answer, and it's lovely.
If you chemically synthesize just the central chromophore, by itself, and dissolve it in water — it does not glow.
It absorbs light fine.
But the energy gets dumped away as molecular vibration and rotation, not as emitted light.
The chromophore wiggles, the excited state collapses non-radiatively, and instead of fluorescence you get a tiny bit of heat.
Useless.
To make the chromophore actually fluoresce, you have to hold it absolutely still.
You have to prevent the internal wiggles that drain away the energy.
And that is exactly what the barrel does.
The chromophore is buried at the center of a rigid, water-excluding cage.
There is nowhere for it to wiggle.
Excited-state energy has no escape route except as emitted light.
The barrel is, essentially, a fluorescence-enforcing scaffold around an otherwise non-luminous chemistry.
The analogy I like for this is: imagine you have a tiny bell.
If you ring it while holding it loosely in your fist, the sound is muffled — your hand absorbs the vibration.
If you mount the bell rigidly on a stand and ring it, the sound rings out clearly.
The chromophore is the bell.
The barrel is the rigid mount.
Without the barrel, the energy gets absorbed away as motion.
With the barrel, the energy rings out as light.
The barrel does a few other jobs worth noting.
It protects the chromophore from quenchers floating in the cytoplasm.
It controls the exact electronic environment around the chromophore — which, we'll see in a moment, is the key to engineering different colors.
And it makes the whole protein remarkably tough.
GFP is heat-stable, protease-resistant, and just generally hard to break.
The barrel is a robust little package.

So the take-home picture: an eleven-stranded beta barrel, the protein soda can, four nanometers tall, hollow on the inside, with three internal amino acids at its heart that spontaneously cook themselves into a fluorescent chromophore.
The barrel keeps the chromophore rigid so its absorbed light gets emitted instead of dissipated.
The whole apparatus folds and matures by itself, in any cell with oxygen.
That is GFP.
Hold that picture in your head; every variant we talk about for the rest of the lecture is some clever modification of it.
